1→# Coding Session -- Architecture Design
     2→
     3→## 1. System Context
     4→
     5→The coding-session feature adds an interactive supervisory layer between Brain's human operator and spawned OpenCode coding agents. It wires existing infrastructure (spawn, event bridge, stall detector, SSE registry) into a live feedback loop.
     6→
     7→### C4 Level 1: System Context
     8→
     9→```mermaid
    10→graph TB
    11→    User["Marcus (Solo Operator)"]
    12→    Brain["Brain Platform"]
    13→    OpenCode["OpenCode Agent Process"]
    14→    GitRepo["Git Repository (Worktree)"]
    15→    SurrealDB["SurrealDB"]
    16→
    17→    User -- "assigns tasks, sends follow-up prompts,\nreviews diffs, accepts/rejects" --> Brain
    18→    Brain -- "spawns, sends prompts,\nreceives event stream" --> OpenCode
    19→    OpenCode -- "edits files in\nisolated worktree" --> GitRepo
    20→    Brain -- "persists sessions,\nconversation logs" --> SurrealDB
    21→    Brain -- "streams agent tokens,\nstatus, file changes via SSE" --> User
    22→```
    23→
    24→### C4 Level 2: Container Diagram
    25→
    26→```mermaid
    27→graph TB
    28→    subgraph Client["Browser Client (Vanilla JS + React)"]
    29→        AgentPanel["AgentSessionPanel"]
    30→        AgentOutput["AgentSessionOutput"]
    31→        PromptInput["FollowUpPromptInput"]
    32→        ReviewPage["Review Page (Diff + Agent Log tabs)"]
    33→        SSEHook["useAgentSession Hook"]
    34→    end
    35→
    36→    subgraph Server["Bun HTTP Server"]
    37→        Routes["Orchestrator Routes"]
    38→        Lifecycle["Session Lifecycle"]
    39→        HandleReg["Handle Registry (in-memory)"]
    40→        EventBridge["Event Bridge"]
    41→        StallDetect["Stall Detector"]
    42→        LogPersist["Conversation Log Persistence"]
    43→        SSEReg["SSE Registry"]
    44→    end
    45→
    46→    subgraph External["External Processes"]
    47→        OpenCode["OpenCode Server Process"]
    48→    end
    49→
    50→    subgraph Data["Data Layer"]
    51→        SurrealDB["SurrealDB"]
    52→    end
    53→
    54→    AgentPanel -- "renders" --> AgentOutput
    55→    AgentPanel -- "renders" --> PromptInput
    56→    SSEHook -- "subscribes via EventSource" --> SSEReg
    57→    PromptInput -- "POST /prompt" --> Routes
    58→    ReviewPage -- "GET /review + GET /log" --> Routes
    59→
    60→    Routes -- "delegates to" --> Lifecycle
    61→    Lifecycle -- "stores/retrieves handles" --> HandleReg
    62→    Lifecycle -- "for-await-of eventStream" --> OpenCode
    63→    Lifecycle -- "pipes events through" --> EventBridge
    64→    EventBridge -- "emits StreamEvents" --> SSEReg
    65→    EventBridge -- "notifies" --> StallDetect
    66→    EventBridge -- "appends log entries" --> LogPersist
    67→    LogPersist -- "persists conversation log" --> SurrealDB
    68→    Lifecycle -- "reads/writes session state" --> SurrealDB
    69→    HandleReg -- "sendPrompt(text)" --> OpenCode
    70→```
    71→
    72→## 2. Component Architecture
    73→
    74→### 2.1 Server Components
    75→
    76→| Component | Location | Responsibility | Status |
    77→|-----------|----------|---------------|--------|
    78→| spawn-opencode | `orchestrator/spawn-opencode.ts` | Spawn OpenCode process, expose handle | **EXISTS** -- complete |
    79→| event-bridge | `orchestrator/event-bridge.ts` | Transform OpenCode events to StreamEvents | **EXISTS** -- complete |
    80→| stall-detector | `orchestrator/stall-detector.ts` | Monitor session activity, abort on stall | **EXISTS** -- complete |
    81→| session-lifecycle | `orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts` | Session CRUD, state machine, handle registry | **EXISTS** -- needs event iteration wiring |
    82→| routes | `orchestrator/routes.ts` | HTTP route handlers | **EXISTS** -- needs prompt endpoint |
    83→| sse-registry | `streaming/sse-registry.ts` | SSE stream management | **EXISTS** -- needs orchestrator wiring |
    84→| conversation-log | `orchestrator/conversation-log.ts` | Persist/retrieve agent conversation log | **NEW** |
    85→
    86→### 2.2 Client Components
    87→
    88→| Component | Location | Responsibility | Status |
    89→|-----------|----------|---------------|--------|
    90→| AgentStatusSection | `components/graph/AgentStatusSection.tsx` | Status badge, assign button | **EXISTS** -- needs panel delegation |
    91→| useAgentSession | `hooks/use-agent-session.ts` | SSE subscription, state reducer | **EXISTS** -- needs token accumulation |
    92→| orchestrator-api | `graph/orchestrator-api.ts` | Client fetch wrappers | **EXISTS** -- needs sendPrompt + getLog |
    93→| AgentSessionOutput | `components/graph/AgentSessionOutput.tsx` | Token stream display, auto-scroll | **NEW** |
    94→| AgentSessionPanel | `components/graph/AgentSessionPanel.tsx` | Composite: output + prompt input | **NEW** |
    95→| AgentLogView | `components/graph/AgentLogView.tsx` | Chronological conversation log for review | **NEW** |
    96→
    97→### 2.3 Shared Contracts
    98→
    99→| Type | Location | Status |
   100→|------|----------|--------|
   101→| AgentTokenEvent | `shared/contracts.ts` | **EXISTS** |
   102→| AgentFileChangeEvent | `shared/contracts.ts` | **EXISTS** |
   103→| AgentStatusEvent | `shared/contracts.ts` | **EXISTS** |
   104→| AgentStallWarningEvent | `shared/contracts.ts` | **EXISTS** |
   105→| AgentPromptEvent | `shared/contracts.ts` | **NEW** -- user prompt echoed in stream |
   106→| ConversationLogEntry | `shared/contracts.ts` | **NEW** -- structured log entry for persistence |
   107→
   108→## 3. Data Flow
   109→
   110→### 3.1 Event Stream Flow (US-CS-003 + US-CS-001)
   111→
   112→```
   113→OpenCode Process
   114→  |
   115→  | AsyncIterable<OpencodeEvent>
   116→  v
   117→createOrchestratorSession (for-await-of loop, fire-and-forget)
   118→  |
   119→  | OpencodeEvent
   120→  v
   121→startEventBridge.handleEvent()
   122→  |-- transformOpencodeEvent() -> StreamEvent
   123→  |-- deps.emitEvent(streamId, streamEvent) -> SSE Registry
   124→  |-- deps.updateLastEventAt(sessionId) -> SurrealDB
   125→  |-- stallDetector.recordActivity()
   126→  |-- appendLogEntry(sessionId, logEntry) -> SurrealDB
   127→  |
   128→  v
   129→SSE Registry -> EventSource -> useAgentSession hook
   130→  |
   131→  | reduceAgentSessionEvent()
   132→  v
   133→AgentSessionOutput (renders accumulated tokens)
   134→```
   135→
   136→### 3.2 Follow-Up Prompt Flow (US-CS-002)
   137→
   138→```
   139→User types in PromptInput
   140→  |
   141→  | POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt { text }
   142→  v
   143→promptSession route handler
   144→  |-- validate session exists and is active
   145→  |-- look up OpenCodeHandle from handleRegistry
   146→  |-- handle.sendPrompt(text) (fire-and-forget)
   147→  |-- emit AgentPromptEvent to SSE stream (echo back to client)
   148→  |-- appendLogEntry(sessionId, { type: "user_prompt", text })
   149→  |
   150→  v
   151→Response: 202 Accepted
   152→```
   153→
   154→### 3.3 Review with Agent Log (US-CS-004)
   155→
   156→```
   157→User navigates to review page
   158→  |
   159→  | GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/review (existing)
   160→  | GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/log (new)
   161→  v
   162→Review page renders two tabs:
   163→  - Diff tab (existing)
   164→  - Agent Log tab (new: chronological conversation trail)
   165→
   166→Reject with Feedback:
   167→  |
   168→  | POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/reject { feedback }
   169→  v
   170→rejectOrchestratorSession (existing)
   171→  |-- sets status to "active"
   172→  |-- handle.sendPrompt(feedback) via handleRegistry
   173→  |-- appendLogEntry(sessionId, { type: "user_prompt", text: feedback })
   174→```
   175→
   176→## 4. Architecture Decisions
   177→
   178→### 4.1 SSE Registry Wiring Gap
   179→
   180→**Finding**: `wireOrchestratorRoutes` is called WITHOUT `sseRegistry` in `start-server.ts`. The SSE registry exists at `deps.sse` but is not passed to the orchestrator wiring.
   181→
   182→**Resolution**: Pass `deps.sse` as `sseRegistry` to `wireOrchestratorRoutes`. Also register the stream in the SSE registry when creating a session (call `sseRegistry.registerMessage(streamId)` before spawning).
   183→
   184→### 4.2 Orchestrator SSE Stream Route
   185→
   186→**Finding**: No stream route is registered for orchestrator sessions. The chat system has `/api/chat/stream/:messageId` but the orchestrator's stream handler (created in `routes.ts` when `sseRegistry` is provided) has no registered URL path.
   187→
   188→**Resolution**: Add route `/api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/sessions/:sessionId/stream` that delegates to the SSE registry's `handleStreamRequest(streamId)`.
   189→
   190→### 4.3 Event Iteration Strategy
   191→
   192→The `for-await-of` loop over `handle.eventStream` runs as a fire-and-forget background task after `createOrchestratorSession` returns. It must:
   193→- Not block the HTTP response
   194→- Catch errors and update session status
   195→- Stop on terminal status events
   196→- Clean up resources on completion
   197→
   198→### 4.4 Token Accumulation in useAgentSession
   199→
   200→The existing hook tracks `status`, `filesChanged`, `stallWarning` but does NOT accumulate `agent_token` events. The hook must be extended to accumulate tokens into a structured array and expose them to `AgentSessionOutput`.
   201→
   202→Token accumulation model: **structured array** (not string concat). Each entry carries the token text and a timestamp, enabling the output component to render inline file-change notifications at the correct position in the stream.
   203→
   204→### 4.5 Conversation Log Persistence
   205→
   206→Server-side persistence (SurrealDB) chosen over client-side session storage. Rationale: log must survive page navigation, browser refresh, and be available on the review page. See ADR-005.
   207→
   208→## 5. Integration Points
   209→
   210→| Integration | Mechanism | Contract |
   211→|-------------|-----------|----------|
   212→| Server -> OpenCode | SDK client (HTTP) | `sendPrompt(text)`, `eventStream` |
   213→| Server -> Client | SSE via `sse-registry` | `StreamEvent` union type |
   214→| Client -> Server | HTTP REST | Orchestrator route handlers |
   215→| Server -> SurrealDB | Surreal SDK | `agent_session` table + new `conversation_log` fields |
   216→| Event Bridge -> SSE | `emitEvent(streamId, event)` | `StreamEvent` discriminated union |
   217→
   218→## 6. Quality Attribute Strategies
   219→
   220→| Attribute | Strategy |
   221→|-----------|----------|
   222→| **Responsiveness** | Fire-and-forget event iteration; 202 for prompt endpoint; SSE for real-time push |
   223→| **Reliability** | Stall detector (existing); SSE reconnection (useAgentSession error handler); session state in DB survives client disconnects |
   224→| **Maintainability** | Pure transform functions (event bridge); dependency injection (function signatures as ports); shared contracts |
   225→| **Testability** | Pure core/effect shell pattern throughout; injectable clock/deps in stall detector; state reducer is pure |
   226→| **Simplicity** | Reuse existing SSE registry, event bridge, stall detector; no new infrastructure components |
   227→
   228→## 7. Architectural Constraints for Implementation
   229→
   230→These are critical wiring requirements the crafter must address:
   231→
   232→1. **SSE Registry must be passed to orchestrator wiring.** In `start-server.ts`, `wireOrchestratorRoutes` is currently called WITHOUT `sseRegistry`. Pass `deps.sse` as `sseRegistry` parameter. Without this, no events reach clients.
   233→2. **SSE stream must be registered before spawn.** Call `sseRegistry.registerMessage(streamId)` in `createOrchestratorSession` before starting the event iteration loop. The stream URL is returned to the client in the assign response -- clients connect immediately.
   234→3. **Orchestrator stream route must be added.** Register `/api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/sessions/:sessionId/stream` in `start-server.ts` route table. The handler delegates to `sseRegistry.handleStreamRequest(streamId)`.
   235→4. **Prompt endpoint inherits existing auth.** The POST /prompt endpoint uses the same Cookie-based auth as all orchestrator endpoints. No new authentication surface.
   236→5. **Event iteration must not block HTTP response.** The `for-await-of` loop is launched as an unlinked async IIFE. See ADR-006.
   237→6. **Token batching for log persistence.** Accumulate tokens in memory, flush at turn boundaries (idle status or user prompt). See ADR-005.
   238→
   239→## 8. Security
   240→
   241→- All orchestrator endpoints are behind existing Cookie-based authentication (extracted in `routes.ts` via `extractAuthToken`)
   242→- The prompt endpoint accepts only `{ text: string }` -- no injection surface beyond what `sendPrompt` already handles
   243→- SSE streams are scoped by `streamId` (UUID-based) -- not guessable
   244→- No new external network surface; OpenCode listens on `127.0.0.1` only
   245→
   246→## 9. Known Limitations
   247→
   248→- **Server restart loses handles**: In-memory `handleRegistry` is ephemeral. Active sessions become orphaned on restart. Documented limitation -- not addressed in this feature.
   249→- **No SSE reconnection for orchestrator streams**: If EventSource drops, client shows "Connection lost" but does not auto-reconnect. The useAgentSession hook closes on error. This matches the existing chat SSE behavior.
   250→- **Conversation log size**: Unbounded for now. Agent sessions are short-lived (minutes to hours). Cap at 10,000 entries if needed later.
   251→

1→# Coding Session -- Component Boundaries
     2→
     3→## Server-Side Boundaries
     4→
     5→### Orchestrator Domain (`app/src/server/orchestrator/`)
     6→
     7→Owns: session lifecycle, agent process management, event forwarding, conversation log persistence.
     8→
     9→```
    10→orchestrator/
    11→  session-lifecycle.ts    [MODIFY] -- add event iteration loop, prompt forwarding, log persistence calls
    12→  routes.ts               [MODIFY] -- add prompt endpoint, log endpoint
    13→  event-bridge.ts         [MODIFY] -- add log entry emission alongside SSE emission
    14→  spawn-opencode.ts       [NO CHANGE] -- already complete
    15→  stall-detector.ts       [NO CHANGE] -- already complete
    16→  conversation-log.ts     [NEW] -- conversation log CRUD (SurrealDB queries)
    17→  types.ts                [MODIFY] -- add ConversationLogEntry type if needed
    18→```
    19→
    20→### Streaming Domain (`app/src/server/streaming/`)
    21→
    22→Owns: SSE stream lifecycle, event delivery to clients.
    23→
    24→```
    25→streaming/
    26→  sse-registry.ts         [NO CHANGE] -- already supports orchestrator event types
    27→```
    28→
    29→### Runtime Wiring (`app/src/server/runtime/`)
    30→
    31→Owns: dependency injection, route registration.
    32→
    33→```
    34→runtime/
    35→  start-server.ts         [MODIFY] -- pass sseRegistry to orchestrator wiring, add stream + prompt + log routes
    36→```
    37→
    38→### Shared Contracts (`app/src/shared/`)
    39→
    40→Owns: types shared between server and client.
    41→
    42→```
    43→shared/
    44→  contracts.ts            [MODIFY] -- add AgentPromptEvent, ConversationLogEntry
    45→```
    46→
    47→## Client-Side Boundaries
    48→
    49→### Agent Session UI (`app/src/client/components/graph/`)
    50→
    51→Owns: agent session visualization, interaction controls.
    52→
    53→```
    54→components/graph/
    55→  AgentStatusSection.tsx  [MODIFY] -- delegate to AgentSessionPanel when session active
    56→  AgentSessionPanel.tsx   [NEW] -- composite: output + prompt input + abort button
    57→  AgentSessionOutput.tsx  [NEW] -- token stream display with auto-scroll
    58→  AgentLogView.tsx        [NEW] -- chronological conversation log for review page
    59→```
    60→
    61→### Hooks (`app/src/client/hooks/`)
    62→
    63→Owns: SSE subscription, state management.
    64→
    65→```
    66→hooks/
    67→  use-agent-session.ts    [MODIFY] -- accumulate tokens + file changes into structured entries
    68→```
    69→
    70→### API Layer (`app/src/client/graph/`)
    71→
    72→Owns: typed fetch wrappers.
    73→
    74→```
    75→graph/
    76→  orchestrator-api.ts     [MODIFY] -- add sendPrompt(), getConversationLog()
    77→```
    78→
    79→## Boundary Rules
    80→
    81→1. **Orchestrator domain does NOT import from chat domain.** SSE registry is the shared integration point, injected via dependency.
    82→2. **Event bridge is the ONLY component that transforms OpenCode events.** All consumers receive `StreamEvent` types.
    83→3. **Handle registry is internal to session-lifecycle.** Routes access handles indirectly through lifecycle functions.
    84→4. **Conversation log queries are isolated in conversation-log.ts.** Session-lifecycle calls log functions but does not construct queries directly.
    85→5. **Client components consume `useAgentSession` hook state.** They do NOT directly interact with EventSource or SSE.
    86→
    87→## File Change Summary
    88→
    89→| Category | Existing Modified | New Files | Total |
    90→|----------|-------------------|-----------|-------|
    91→| Server | 4 | 1 | 5 |
    92→| Client | 3 | 3 | 6 |
    93→| Shared | 1 | 0 | 1 |
    94→| Runtime | 1 | 0 | 1 |
    95→| **Total** | **9** | **4** | **13** |
    96→
    97→Step ratio check: 6 steps / 13 production files = 0.46 (well under 2.5 threshold).
    98→

1→# Coding Session -- Data Models
     2→
     3→## Existing Schema (No Changes Required)
     4→
     5→The `agent_session` table already has all fields needed for session lifecycle:
     6→
     7→```
     8→agent_session (SCHEMAFULL)
     9→  agent: string
    10→  started_at: datetime
    11→  ended_at: option<datetime>
    12→  workspace: record<workspace>
    13→  task_id: option<record<task>>
    14→  orchestrator_status: option<string>  -- "spawning" | "active" | "idle" | "completed" | "aborted" | "error"
    15→  worktree_branch: option<string>
    16→  worktree_path: option<string>
    17→  stream_id: option<string>
    18→  opencode_session_id: option<string>
    19→  last_event_at: option<datetime>
    20→  last_feedback: option<string>
    21→  error_message: option<string>
    22→  files_changed: option<array<object>>
    23→  ...
    24→```
    25→
    26→## New Schema: Conversation Log
    27→
    28→New field on `agent_session` to store the conversation log inline. Simpler than a separate table -- each session has exactly one log, entries are always accessed together, and sessions are short-lived.
    29→
    30→### Schema Migration
    31→
    32→```
    33→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log ON agent_session TYPE option<array<object>>;
    34→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log[*].entry_type ON agent_session TYPE string
    35→  ASSERT $value IN ["agent_text", "file_change", "user_prompt", "status_change", "stall_warning"];
    36→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log[*].timestamp ON agent_session TYPE datetime;
    37→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log[*].content ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    38→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log[*].file ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    39→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log[*].change_type ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    40→DEFINE FIELD conversation_log[*].status ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    41→```
    42→
    43→### Log Entry Types
    44→
    45→| entry_type | content | file | change_type | status | When |
    46→|-----------|---------|------|-------------|--------|------|
    47→| `agent_text` | Token text (batched) | - | - | - | On `agent_token` events (batched per message turn) |
    48→| `file_change` | - | File path | created/modified/deleted | - | On `agent_file_change` event |
    49→| `user_prompt` | Prompt text | - | - | - | On POST /prompt or reject-with-feedback |
    50→| `status_change` | - | - | - | active/idle/completed/error/aborted | On `agent_status` event |
    51→| `stall_warning` | Warning message | - | - | - | On stall detection |
    52→
    53→### Token Batching Strategy
    54→
    55→`agent_token` events arrive as individual tokens (words/fragments). Appending each as a separate log entry would create thousands of entries. Instead:
    56→
    57→- Accumulate tokens in memory during a message turn
    58→- Flush as a single `agent_text` log entry when:
    59→  - A `status_change` to `idle` occurs (turn complete)
    60→  - A `user_prompt` is received (new turn boundary)
    61→  - A configurable flush interval elapses (30s safety net)
    62→
    63→This keeps the log at ~10-50 entries per session instead of thousands.
    64→
    65→## Shared Contract Types
    66→
    67→### AgentPromptEvent (new StreamEvent variant)
    68→
    69→```
    70→type: "agent_prompt"
    71→sessionId: string
    72→text: string
    73→```
    74→
    75→Emitted to SSE when a user sends a follow-up prompt. Allows the client to render user messages inline in the output stream without polling.
    76→
    77→### ConversationLogEntry (new shared type)
    78→
    79→```
    80→entryType: "agent_text" | "file_change" | "user_prompt" | "status_change" | "stall_warning"
    81→timestamp: string (ISO)
    82→content?: string
    83→file?: string
    84→changeType?: string
    85→status?: string
    86→```
    87→
    88→Used in the GET /log response and by the AgentLogView component.
    89→
    90→## Client-Side State
    91→
    92→### Extended AgentSessionState
    93→
    94→The `useAgentSession` hook state adds:
    95→
    96→- `tokens: Array<{ text: string; timestamp: string }>` -- accumulated token stream entries
    97→- `fileChanges: Array<{ file: string; changeType: string; timestamp: string }>` -- inline file change notifications
    98→- `userPrompts: Array<{ text: string; timestamp: string }>` -- echoed user prompts
    99→
   100→These are interleaved by timestamp in the AgentSessionOutput component for chronological display.
   101→
   102→## API Contracts
   103→
   104→### POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt
   105→
   106→Request: `{ text: string }`
   107→Response: `202 Accepted` (empty body)
   108→Errors: 404 (session not found), 409 (terminal status)
   109→
   110→### GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/log
   111→
   112→Response: `{ entries: ConversationLogEntry[] }`
   113→Errors: 404 (session not found)
   114→
   115→Returns the persisted conversation log for the review page. The live session uses SSE events directly; this endpoint is for after-the-fact review.
   116→

1→# FP Principles
     2→
     3→Core functional programming thinking patterns. Language-agnostic.
     4→
     5→Cross-references: [fp-domain-modeling](./fp-domain-modeling.md) | [fp-hexagonal-architecture](./fp-hexagonal-architecture.md) | [fp-algebra-driven-design](./fp-algebra-driven-design.md)
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. Higher-Order Functions as Problem Decomposition
    10→
    11→[STARTER]
    12→
    13→Three operations replace most loops:
    14→
    15→| Operation | Purpose | Replaces |
    16→|-----------|---------|----------|
    17→| **Map** | Transform each element, preserve structure | Loop building new collection |
    18→| **Filter** | Keep elements matching condition | Loop with conditional |
    19→| **Fold** | Accumulate elements into single result | Loop with running total |
    20→
    21→**When to use Map**: Transform every element without changing collection shape. Nested maps handle nested structures.
    22→**When to use Filter**: Select elements without changing their values.
    23→**When to use Fold**: Reduce collection to single value. Accumulator IS your state. Combining function IS your state transition. Folds make state machines explicit.
    24→
    25→**Decision**: "Am I transforming, selecting, or accumulating?" Pick matching operation. If none fit, compose two.
    26→
    27→**Why**: These operations communicate intent. Map says "same shape, different values." Fold says "many inputs, one output." Loops say nothing about intent until you read every line.
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## 2. Type-Driven Design
    32→
    33→[STARTER]
    34→
    35→Write the type signature before implementation. The type tells you what the function can and cannot do.
    36→
    37→**Process**:
    38→1. Declare what the function consumes and produces
    39→2. Ask: "which type-specific operations do I actually use?"
    40→3. Replace concrete types with type variables for everything you don't inspect
    41→4. Add constraints only for capabilities you use (equality, ordering, display)
    42→
    43→**Design progression**: Concrete types -> type variables -> constrained type variables. Each step increases reuse while documenting minimal assumptions.
    44→
    45→**Why**: Function's type signature is a contract. Narrower types mean fewer possible implementations, fewer bugs.
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→## 3. Pattern Matching as Decision Decomposition
    50→
    51→[STARTER]
    52→
    53→Decompose decisions by data shape, not boolean conditions. Each clause handles one concrete case. Compiler verifies exhaustiveness.
    54→
    55→**When to use pattern matching**: "What shape is this data?"
    56→**When to use guards/conditions**: "What property does this value have?"
    57→**When to use named bindings**: Intermediate results need a name to avoid repetition.
    58→
    59→**Heuristic**: Prefer small extracted functions over giant match expressions. Pattern match on top-level shape, delegate to named functions for sub-decisions.
    60→
    61→**Exhaustiveness as safety net**: When you add a new variant to a choice type, compiler flags every match that doesn't handle it.
    62→
    63→---
    64→
    65→## 4. Composition Patterns
    66→
    67→[INTERMEDIATE]
    68→
    69→### Partial Application
    70→
    71→Fix some arguments of a general function to create specialized version. Eliminates throwaway helper functions.
    72→
    73→**When**: General function exists and you need specialized version for specific context.
    74→
    75→### Function Composition (Pipelines)
    76→
    77→Chain functions where output of one feeds into next. Each function has single responsibility.
    78→
    79→**Why**: Composition reveals architecture of computation. Pipelines read as sequence of steps, making business process visible.
    80→
    81→### Point-Free Style
    82→
    83→Omit explicit argument when function is just a composition. Use when it reveals intent. Avoid when it obscures meaning.
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→## 5. Container Abstractions
    88→
    89→[INTERMEDIATE] -> [ADVANCED]
    90→
    91→Progressive hierarchy for working with values inside containers (nullables, lists, futures, results).
    92→
    93→### [INTERMEDIATE] Transformable Container (Functor)
    94→
    95→**What**: Apply function to values inside container without changing structure.
    96→**Plain English**: "I have a value in a box. Transform the value without opening the box."
    97→**When**: You have nullable/optional/list/future and want to transform contents without inspecting the container.
    98→**Guarantees**: Transforming with identity does nothing. Can fuse or split transformations freely.
    99→
   100→### [INTERMEDIATE] Combinable Containers (Applicative)
   101→
   102→**What**: Apply a function inside a container to values inside other containers.
   103→**Plain English**: "I have a function in a box AND values in boxes. Combine them."
   104→**When**: Validation -- check multiple fields independently, combine results only if all succeed. Doesn't short-circuit; collects all errors.
   105→
   106→### [INTERMEDIATE] Combinable Values (Monoid)
   107→
   108→**What**: Combine two values of same type into one, with default element that changes nothing.
   109→**Plain English**: "I have many values. Smash them together into one."
   110→**When**: Folding/reducing collections. Combining operation must be associative, enabling parallelism.
   111→**Examples**: String concatenation with empty string | addition with zero | list append with empty list.
   112→
   113→### [ADVANCED] Chainable Operations (Monad)
   114→
   115→**What**: Chain operations where each step produces wrapped value, next step depends on previous result.
   116→**Plain English**: "Step 1's output determines what step 2 does. Each step might fail/branch/have effects."
   117→**When**: Sequential dependent operations where each step can fail, branch, or produce effects.
   118→
   119→### Decision Tree: Which Abstraction Do I Need?
   120→
   121→```
   122→Do I need to transform values inside a container?
   123→  YES, one function, one container --> Transformable (Functor)
   124→  YES, combine multiple independent containers --> Combinable Containers (Applicative)
   125→  YES, chain dependent operations sequentially --> Chainable Operations (Monad)
   126→Do I need to combine values of the same type?
   127→  YES --> Combinable Values (Monoid)
   128→```
   129→
   130→### Progression Summary
   131→
   132→Each level adds a new kind of combination:
   133→- **Transformable**: one function, one container
   134→- **Combinable Containers**: one function, multiple containers (independent)
   135→- **Chainable**: sequential dependent operations, each producing container
   136→- **Combinable Values**: same-type values collapsed into one
   137→
   138→### Runnable Example: Map, Filter, Fold on Domain Objects
   139→
   140→```
   141→orders = [Order(100, "pending"), Order(250, "shipped"), Order(50, "pending")]
   142→
   143→pendingTotals = orders
   144→  |> filter (o -> o.status == "pending")    -- [Order(100, "pending"), Order(50, "pending")]
   145→  |> map (o -> o.amount)                    -- [100, 50]
   146→  |> fold 0 (acc, x -> acc + x)            -- 150
   147→```
   148→
   149→---
   150→
   151→## 6. Specialized Chainable Patterns
   152→
   153→[ADVANCED]
   154→
   155→| Pattern | What It Manages | When to Use |
   156→|---------|----------------|-------------|
   157→| **Optional** (Maybe/Option) | Possible absence | Operations that can fail without explanation |
   158→| **Result** (Either) | Failure with context | Operations that fail with error details |
   159→| **Environment** (Reader) | Shared read-only config | Dependency injection, configuration threading |
   160→| **Accumulator** (Writer) | Side-channel output | Logging, auditing, collecting metadata |
   161→| **Stateful** (State) | Sequential state changes | Counters, parsers, accumulators |
   162→
   163→These compose: real applications stack multiple patterns. See [fp-hexagonal-architecture](./fp-hexagonal-architecture.md) for DI patterns.
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→## 7. Lazy Evaluation as Design Pattern
   168→
   169→[INTERMEDIATE]
   170→
   171→Separate WHAT to compute from WHEN it gets computed. Define potentially infinite sequences and let consumer determine how much to evaluate.
   172→
   173→**When**: Generating candidates then selecting results | pagination and streaming | decoupling producers from consumers | build systems that only rebuild what changed.
   174→
   175→**Separation principle**: Generate all possibilities, then filter. Declarative style says WHAT you want, not HOW to search.
   176→
   177→---
   178→
   179→## 8. The FP Problem-Solving Method
   180→
   181→[STARTER]
   182→
   183→1. **Start with type signature**: What does this function consume and produce?
   184→2. **Identify traversal pattern**: Map, filter, fold, or search?
   185→3. **Recognize accumulator**: If folding, what is the state and how does each element change it?
   186→4. **Decompose by data shape**: Pattern match on constructors, handle each case independently
   187→5. **Compose small functions**: Build complex behavior from simple, tested pieces
   188→
   189→**Mindset shift**: Describe WHAT to compute (transformations, compositions, constraints) rather than HOW (loops, mutations, control flow).
   190→
   191→| Imperative Thinking | Functional Thinking |
   192→|---------------------|---------------------|
   193→| Loop through items | Map/filter/fold over collections |
   194→| Mutate variables | Transform immutable values |
   195→| Check conditions with if/else | Pattern match on data shapes |
   196→| Inherit from base class | Satisfy capability constraints |
   197→| Call methods on objects | Compose functions into pipelines |
   198→| Handle errors with try/catch | Use Optional/Result for explicit failure in types |
   199→| Pass dependencies explicitly | Use Environment pattern for implicit config |
   200→

1→# FP Hexagonal Architecture
     2→
     3→Ports and adapters in functional programming. Structure applications with a pure core and side-effect shell.
     4→
     5→Cross-references: [fp-principles](./fp-principles.md) | [fp-domain-modeling](./fp-domain-modeling.md) | [fp-usable-design](./fp-usable-design.md)
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. The Natural Fit
    10→
    11→[STARTER]
    12→
    13→Functional architecture naturally implements ports and adapters. The paradigm's separation of pure functions from side effects IS the hexagonal boundary.
    14→
    15→| OOP Concept | FP Equivalent | Why |
    16→|---|---|---|
    17→| Port (interface) | Function type signature / type alias | Port defines contract; function signature IS that contract |
    18→| Adapter (class) | Concrete function implementation | Adapter fulfills contract; matching function does same |
    19→| DI container | Function parameters / partial application | Dependencies passed as arguments, no container needed |
    20→| Domain service class | Module of pure functions | Related pure functions replace stateful service object |
    21→| Entity with behavior | Immutable data + functions operating on it | Data and behavior separated; functions transform immutable values |
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## 2. Pure Core / Side-Effect Shell
    26→
    27→[STARTER]
    28→
    29→All business logic is pure; all side effects live at the system's edges.
    30→
    31→**The Sandwich Pattern**: Read (impure) -> Decide (pure) -> Write (impure)
    32→
    33→```
    34→+--------------------------------------------------+
    35→|  Side-Effect Shell (thin)                        |
    36→|  - HTTP handlers, CLI, message consumers         |
    37→|  - Database access, file I/O, network calls      |
    38→|  - Reads data, calls core, writes results        |
    39→|                                                  |
    40→|  +--------------------------------------------+ |
    41→|  |  Pure Core (large)                          | |
    42→|  |  - Pure functions only                      | |
    43→|  |  - Domain logic, validation, calculation    | |
    44→|  |  - No I/O, no side effects                  | |
    45→|  |  - Immutable data transformations           | |
    46→|  +--------------------------------------------+ |
    47→+--------------------------------------------------+
    48→```
    49→
    50→**Dependency Rule**: Shell may call core. Core never calls shell. Core is unaware of shell's existence.
    51→
    52→**Why**: Pure core is trivially testable (no mocks, no setup, no teardown). Shell is thin and needs few integration tests.
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## 3. Ports as Function Types
    57→
    58→[STARTER]
    59→
    60→A port is a function type signature describing a capability the domain needs:
    61→
    62→```
    63→FindOrder    : OrderId -> AsyncResult<Order option>
    64→SaveOrder    : Order -> AsyncResult<unit>
    65→SendEmail    : Email -> AsyncResult<unit>
    66→GetPrice     : ProductCode -> Price
    67→CheckExists  : ProductCode -> bool
    68→```
    69→
    70→**When to define**: Domain needs a capability involving I/O or external systems. Domain declares WHAT; adapter provides HOW.
    71→
    72→**Naming**: Verb-noun. Name describes capability, not technology.
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## 4. Adapters as Implementations
    77→
    78→[STARTER]
    79→
    80→An adapter is a concrete function matching a port's type signature:
    81→
    82→```
    83→PostgresOrderRepo.findOrder  : OrderId -> AsyncResult<Order option>
    84→InMemoryOrderRepo.findOrder  : OrderId -> AsyncResult<Order option>
    85→```
    86→
    87→Both match the `FindOrder` port. Domain doesn't know which is used.
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## 5. Dependency Injection via Functions
    92→
    93→[STARTER] -> [INTERMEDIATE] -> [ADVANCED]
    94→
    95→### Decision Tree: How to Inject This Dependency?
    96→
    97→```
    98→How many dependencies does the function need?
    99→  1-3 --> [STARTER] Functions as Parameters
   100→  4-6 --> [INTERMEDIATE] Consider Environment Pattern or grouping
   101→  7+  --> [ADVANCED] Capability Interfaces or Effect System
   102→         (also: reconsider function responsibilities)
   103→```
   104→
   105→### [STARTER] Functions as Parameters
   106→
   107→Pass dependencies as function parameters. Partially apply at composition root.
   108→
   109→```
   110→placeOrder (findCustomer) (saveOrder) (rawOrder) = ...
   111→placeOrderHandler = placeOrder Database.findCustomer Database.saveOrder
   112→```
   113→
   114→### [INTERMEDIATE] Environment Pattern (Reader)
   115→
   116→Dependencies in a record, provided once at top level. Use when parameter threading becomes painful (4+ deps).
   117→
   118→```
   119→placeOrder (rawOrder) = reader { env = ask(); env.findCustomer(rawOrder.customerId) ... }
   120→placeOrder(rawOrder) |> runWith(productionEnv)
   121→```
   122→
   123→### [ADVANCED] Capability Interfaces / Effect Systems
   124→
   125→Abstract over effect types (tagless final) or use fine-grained effect tracking (ZIO, Koka). Use for large codebases with many effects.
   126→
   127→### Recommendation by Context
   128→
   129→| Context | Approach |
   130→|---|---|
   131→| Small/medium codebase | Functions as parameters |
   132→| Large codebase, many effects | Capability interfaces or effect system |
   133→| Pragmatic TypeScript/F# | Functions as parameters + modules |
   134→
   135→---
   136→
   137→## 6. Pipeline Composition Through Architecture
   138→
   139→[INTERMEDIATE]
   140→
   141→Workflows flow through architecture as pipelines:
   142→
   143→```
   144→HTTP Request
   145→  -> Parse (shell: impure)
   146→  -> Validate (core: pure)
   147→  -> Calculate (core: pure)
   148→  -> Persist (shell: impure)
   149→  -> Respond (shell: impure)
   150→```
   151→
   152→Each pure step is a function in the pipeline. Shell handles I/O at start and end.
   153→
   154→**Error-track pipelines**: Each step returns Result type; pipeline short-circuits on first failure. See [fp-domain-modeling](./fp-domain-modeling.md).
   155→
   156→**Collect-all-errors**: When you need ALL validation errors, use Applicative style. See [fp-principles](./fp-principles.md) section 5.
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→## 7. Testing Strategy
   161→
   162→[INTERMEDIATE]
   163→
   164→| Layer | Test Type | Volume | Speed | Mocks |
   165→|---|---|---|---|---|
   166→| Pure core (domain) | Unit + Property-based | Many | Fast (ms) | None |
   167→| Composition root | Integration (wiring) | Few | Medium | None |
   168→| Adapters | Integration | Few per adapter | Slow | None (real deps) |
   169→| End-to-end | System tests | Very few | Slowest | None |
   170→
   171→**Key insight**: Pure functions need no mocking. Input in, output out. Strongest practical argument for maximizing the pure core.
   172→
   173→**Property-based testing** is the natural companion. Define rules that hold for all valid inputs. See [fp-algebra-driven-design](./fp-algebra-driven-design.md).
   174→
   175→---
   176→
   177→## 8. Side Effect Management Approaches
   178→
   179→[ADVANCED]
   180→
   181→| Approach | Enforcement | Granularity | Best For |
   182→|---|---|---|---|
   183→| Convention (discipline) | None | N/A | Any language, small teams |
   184→| IO Type (Haskell) | Compile-time | Binary (pure/impure) | Haskell |
   185→| Effect Systems (ZIO, Koka) | Compile-time | Per-effect | Large systems |
   186→| Pure Core / Shell | Architectural | Module-level | Any language, pragmatic |
   187→
   188→**IO actions as values**: Side effects are descriptions of actions, not actions themselves. Can be stored, composed, and only execute when runtime reaches them.
   189→
   190→**Type-level effect tracking**: Mark impure functions clearly -- through return types, naming conventions, or annotations. Even without compiler enforcement, the discipline applies.
   191→
   192→---
   193→
   194→## 9. Combining Patterns
   195→
   196→```
   197→Domain Wrappers + Smart Constructors (fp-domain-modeling)
   198→        |
   199→        v
   200→Choice Types for State Machines -----> Error-Track Pipelines
   201→        |                                       |
   202→        v                                       v
   203→Pure Core / Side-Effect Shell ---------> Functions as Parameters (DI)
   204→        |                                       |
   205→        v                                       v
   206→Pipeline Composition <-----------------  Property-Based Testing
   207→```
   208→
   209→### Worked Example: Place Order Workflow
   210→
   211→```
   212→-- Ports (function signatures)
   213→FindCustomer : CustomerId -> AsyncResult<Customer>
   214→SaveOrder    : Order -> AsyncResult<Unit>
   215→
   216→-- Pure Core (domain logic)
   217→validateOrder : RawOrder -> Result<ValidOrder, ValidationError>
   218→priceOrder    : ValidOrder -> PricedOrder
   219→
   220→-- Pipeline (Pure Core + Error Pipeline + DI via parameters)
   221→placeOrder (findCustomer) (saveOrder) (raw) =
   222→    raw
   223→    |> validateOrder                     -- pure, Result
   224→    |> bindAsync (o -> findCustomer o.customerId |> map (c -> (o, c)))  -- port call
   225→    |> map (fun (o, c) -> priceOrder o)  -- pure
   226→    |> bindAsync saveOrder               -- port call
   227→```
   228→
   229→**Recommended learning sequence**:
   230→
   231→[STARTER]: Pure Core/Shell -> Domain Wrappers -> Smart Constructors -> Pipeline Composition
   232→
   233→[INTERMEDIATE]: Choice Types -> Error-Track Pipelines -> Functions as Parameters -> Property Testing
   234→
   235→[ADVANCED]: Capability Interfaces -> Effect Systems -> Collect-All-Errors Validation
   236→

- Stall detector starts monitoring activity on the new session
- Session status transitions to active after first agent event received
- Event iteration stops on session abort or terminal status

Execute in order:
0. PREPARE - Load context, verify prerequisites, read existing source files and design docs
1. RED_ACCEPTANCE - Write failing acceptance test
2. RED_UNIT - Write failing unit test
3. GREEN - Minimal code to pass tests
   After GREEN: run FULL test suite. If all pass, proceed to COMMIT immediately.
   Never move to new task or stop without committing green code.
4. COMMIT - Stage and commit with conventional message
   Include git trailer: `Step-ID: 01-02` (required for DES verification)

- All tests pass before COMMIT
- No skipped phases without blocked_by reason

1→# Coding Session -- Architecture Design
     2→
     3→## 1. System Context
     4→
     5→The coding-session feature adds an interactive supervisory layer between Brain's human operator and spawned OpenCode coding agents. It wires existing infrastructure (spawn, event bridge, stall detector, SSE registry) into a live feedback loop.
     6→
     7→### C4 Level 1: System Context
     8→
     9→```mermaid
    10→graph TB
    11→    User["Marcus (Solo Operator)"]
    12→    Brain["Brain Platform"]
    13→    OpenCode["OpenCode Agent Process"]
    14→    GitRepo["Git Repository (Worktree)"]
    15→    SurrealDB["SurrealDB"]
    16→
    17→    User -- "assigns tasks, sends follow-up prompts,\nreviews diffs, accepts/rejects" --> Brain
    18→    Brain -- "spawns, sends prompts,\nreceives event stream" --> OpenCode
    19→    OpenCode -- "edits files in\nisolated worktree" --> GitRepo
    20→    Brain -- "persists sessions,\nconversation logs" --> SurrealDB
    21→    Brain -- "streams agent tokens,\nstatus, file changes via SSE" --> User
    22→```

   108→## 3. Data Flow
   109→
   110→### 3.1 Event Stream Flow (US-CS-003 + US-CS-001)
   111→
   112→```
   113→OpenCode Process
   114→  |
   115→  | AsyncIterable<OpencodeEvent>
   116→  v
   117→createOrchestratorSession (for-await-of loop, fire-and-forget)
   118→  |
   119→  | OpencodeEvent
   120→  v
   121→startEventBridge.handleEvent()
   122→  |-- transformOpencodeEvent() -> StreamEvent
   123→  |-- deps.emitEvent(streamId, streamEvent) -> SSE Registry
   124→  |-- deps.updateLastEventAt(sessionId) -> SurrealDB
   125→  |-- stallDetector.recordActivity()
   126→  |-- appendLogEntry(sessionId, logEntry) -> SurrealDB
   127→  |
   128→  v
   129→SSE Registry -> EventSource -> useAgentSession hook
   130→  |
   131→  | reduceAgentSessionEvent()
   132→  v
   133→AgentSessionOutput (renders accumulated tokens)
   134→```
   135→
   136→### 3.2 Follow-Up Prompt Flow (US-CS-002)
   137→
   138→```
   139→User types in PromptInput
   140→  |
   141→  | POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt { text }
   142→  v
   143→promptSession route handler
   144→  |-- validate session exists and is active
   145→  |-- look up OpenCodeHandle from handleRegistry
   146→  |-- handle.sendPrompt(text) (fire-and-forget)
   147→  |-- emit AgentPromptEvent to SSE stream (echo back to client)
   148→  |-- appendLogEntry(sessionId, { type: "user_prompt", text })
   149→  |
   150→  v
   151→Response: 202 Accepted
   152→```
   153→
   154→### 3.3 Review with Agent Log (US-CS-004)
   155→
   156→```
   157→User navigates to review page
   158→  |
   159→  | GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/review (existing)
   160→  | GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/log (new)
   161→  v
   162→Review page renders two tabs:
   163→  - Diff tab (existing)
   164→  - Agent Log tab (new: chronological conversation trail)
   165→
   166→Reject with Feedback:
   167→  |
   168→  | POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/reject { feedback }
   169→  v
   170→rejectOrchestratorSession (existing)
   171→  |-- sets status to "active"
   172→  |-- handle.sendPrompt(feedback) via handleRegistry
   173→  |-- appendLogEntry(sessionId, { type: "user_prompt", text: feedback })
   174→```
   175→
   176→## 4. Architecture Decisions
   177→
   178→### 4.1 SSE Registry Wiring Gap
   179→
   180→**Finding**: `wireOrchestratorRoutes` is called WITHOUT `sseRegistry` in `start-server.ts`. The SSE registry exists at `deps.sse` but is not passed to the orchestrator wiring.
   181→
   182→**Resolution**: Pass `deps.sse` as `sseRegistry` to `wireOrchestratorRoutes`. Also register the stream in the SSE registry when creating a session (call `sseRegistry.registerMessage(streamId)` before spawning).
   183→
   184→### 4.2 Orchestrator SSE Stream Route
   185→
   186→**Finding**: No stream route is registered for orchestrator sessions. The chat system has `/api/chat/stream/:messageId` but the orchestrator's stream handler (created in `routes.ts` when `sseRegistry` is provided) has no registered URL path.
   187→
   188→**Resolution**: Add route `/api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/sessions/:sessionId/stream` that delegates to the SSE registry's `handleStreamRequest(streamId)`.
   189→
   190→### 4.3 Event Iteration Strategy
   191→
   192→The `for-await-of` loop over `handle.eventStream` runs as a fire-and-forget background task after `createOrchestratorSession` returns. It must:
   193→- Not block the HTTP response
   194→- Catch errors and update session status
   195→- Stop on terminal status events
   196→- Clean up resources on completion
   197→
   198→### 4.4 Token Accumulation in useAgentSession
   199→
   200→The existing hook tracks `status`, `filesChanged`, `stallWarning` but does NOT accumulate `agent_token` events. The hook must be extended to accumulate tokens into a structured array and expose them to `AgentSessionOutput`.
   201→
   202→Token accumulation model: **structured array** (not string concat). Each entry carries the token text and a timestamp, enabling the output component to render inline file-change notifications at the correct position in the stream.
   203→
   204→### 4.5 Conversation Log Persistence
   205→
   206→Server-side persistence (SurrealDB) chosen over client-side session storage. Rationale: log must survive page navigation, browser refresh, and be available on the review page. See ADR-005.
   207→
   208→## 5. Integration Points
   209→
   210→| Integration | Mechanism | Contract |
   211→|-------------|-----------|----------|
   212→| Server -> OpenCode | SDK client (HTTP) | `sendPrompt(text)`, `eventStream` |
   213→| Server -> Client | SSE via `sse-registry` | `StreamEvent` union type |
   214→| Client -> Server | HTTP REST | Orchestrator route handlers |
   215→| Server -> SurrealDB | Surreal SDK | `agent_session` table + new `conversation_log` fields |
   216→| Event Bridge -> SSE | `emitEvent(streamId, event)` | `StreamEvent` discriminated union |
   217→
   218→## 6. Quality Attribute Strategies
   219→
   220→| Attribute | Strategy |
   221→|-----------|----------|
   222→| **Responsiveness** | Fire-and-forget event iteration; 202 for prompt endpoint; SSE for real-time push |
   223→| **Reliability** | Stall detector (existing); SSE reconnection (useAgentSession error handler); session state in DB survives client disconnects |
   224→| **Maintainability** | Pure transform functions (event bridge); dependency injection (function signatures as ports); shared contracts |
   225→| **Testability** | Pure core/effect shell pattern throughout; injectable clock/deps in stall detector; state reducer is pure |
   226→| **Simplicity** | Reuse existing SSE registry, event bridge, stall detector; no new infrastructure components |
   227→
   228→## 7. Architectural Constraints for Implementation
   229→
   230→These are critical wiring requirements the crafter must address:
   231→
   232→1. **SSE Registry must be passed to orchestrator wiring.** In `start-server.ts`, `wireOrchestratorRoutes` is currently called WITHOUT `sseRegistry`. Pass `deps.sse` as `sseRegistry` parameter. Without this, no events reach clients.
   233→2. **SSE stream must be registered before spawn.** Call `sseRegistry.registerMessage(streamId)` in `createOrchestratorSession` before starting the event iteration loop. The stream URL is returned to the client in the assign response -- clients connect immediately.
   234→3. **Orchestrator stream route must be added.** Register `/api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/sessions/:sessionId/stream` in `start-server.ts` route table. The handler delegates to `sseRegistry.handleStreamRequest(streamId)`.
   235→4. **Prompt endpoint inherits existing auth.** The POST /prompt endpoint uses the same Cookie-based auth as all orchestrator endpoints. No new authentication surface.
   236→5. **Event iteration must not block HTTP response.** The `for-await-of` loop is launched as an unlinked async IIFE. See ADR-006.
   237→6. **Token batching for log persistence.** Accumulate tokens in memory, flush at turn boundaries (idle status or user prompt). See ADR-005.
   238→
   239→## 8. Security
   240→
   241→- All orchestrator endpoints are behind existing Cookie-based authentication (extracted in `routes.ts` via `extractAuthToken`)
   242→- The prompt endpoint accepts only `{ text: string }` -- no injection surface beyond what `sendPrompt` already handles
   243→- SSE streams are scoped by `streamId` (UUID-based) -- not guessable
   244→- No new external network surface; OpenCode listens on `127.0.0.1` only
   245→
   246→## 9. Known Limitations
   247→
   248→- **Server restart loses handles**: In-memory `handleRegistry` is ephemeral. Active sessions become orphaned on restart. Documented limitation -- not addressed in this feature.
   249→- **No SSE reconnection for orchestrator streams**: If EventSource drops, client shows "Connection lost" but does not auto-reconnect. The useAgentSession hook closes on error. This matches the existing chat SSE behavior.
   250→- **Conversation log size**: Unbounded for now. Agent sessions are short-lived (minutes to hours). Cap at 10,000 entries if needed later.
   251→

    79→## Boundary Rules
    80→
    81→1. **Orchestrator domain does NOT import from chat domain.** SSE registry is the shared integration point, injected via dependency.
    82→2. **Event bridge is the ONLY component that transforms OpenCode events.** All consumers receive `StreamEvent` types.
    83→3. **Handle registry is internal to session-lifecycle.** Routes access handles indirectly through lifecycle functions.
    84→4. **Conversation log queries are isolated in conversation-log.ts.** Session-lifecycle calls log functions but does not construct queries directly.
    85→5. **Client components consume `useAgentSession` hook state.** They do NOT directly interact with EventSource or SSE.

1→---
     2→name: tdd-methodology
     3→description: Deep knowledge for Outside-In TDD - double-loop architecture, ATDD integration, port-to-port testing, walking skeletons, and test doubles policy
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Outside-In TDD Methodology
     7→
     8→## Double-Loop TDD Architecture
     9→
    10→Outer loop: ATDD/E2E Tests (customer view) - business requirements, hours-days to green.
    11→Inner loop: Unit Tests (developer view) - technical implementation, minutes to green, RED->GREEN->REFACTOR.
    12→
    13→Outer stays red while inner cycles. Outer drives WHAT to build, inner drives HOW.
    14→Never build components not needed by actual user scenarios.
    15→
    16→## Outside-In vs Inside-Out
    17→
    18→Inside-Out (Classic/bottom-up): discovers collaborators through refactoring. TDD guides design completely.
    19→Outside-In (London/top-down/mockist): knows collaborators upfront, mocks them, implements each moving inward.
    20→
    21→Use Outside-In when: architectural boundaries known (hexagonal), program to interface not implementation.
    22→
    23→## ATDD Integration (Lightweight)
    24→
    25→Original 2008 heavyweight ATDD was "too heavyweight for most real teams." Updated approach (Hendrickson 2024):
    26→- Few Given/When/Then examples, not many | Separate requirements from tests
    27→- Smallest subset of team with relevant skills | Value = shared understanding, not executable specs
    28→- Automate only where high-value
    29→
    30→## BDD Integration
    31→
    32→BDD emerged from Outside-In TDD. Given(context)->When(action)->Then(outcome) maps to outside-in mindset.
    33→BDD reframes TDD as design/specification technique, not just testing. More accessible to stakeholders.
    34→Gherkin: structured format bridging technical/non-technical. Use pragmatically - automate only where high value.
    35→
    36→## Outside-In Development Workflow (Bache)
    37→1. Write Guiding Test (acceptance) from user perspective - thick slice of functionality
    38→2. Start at top-level entry point, design collaborating classes incrementally
    39→3. Use mocks to experiment with interfaces/protocols
    40→4. As each layer implemented, move to previously mocked collaborators, TDD again
    41→5. Never build what isn't needed for actual user scenarios
    42→
    43→## Port-to-Port Testing
    44→
    45→Tests enter through driving port (application service / public API) and assert outcomes at driven port boundaries.
    46→Internal classes (entities, value objects, domain services) exercised indirectly - never instantiated directly in test code.
    47→
    48→Flow: Driving Port -> Application -> Domain -> Driven Port (mocked)
    49→
    50→```python
    51→def test_order_service_processes_payment():
    52→    # Setup - mock driven port (external dependency)
    53→    payment_gateway = MockPaymentGateway()
    54→    order_repo = InMemoryOrderRepository()
    55→
    56→    # Test through driving port (application service)
    57→    order_service = OrderService(payment_gateway, order_repo)
    58→    result = order_service.place_order(customer_id, items)
    59→
    60→    # Assert observable outcomes
    61→    assert result.is_confirmed()
    62→    payment_gateway.verify_charge_called(amount=100.00)
    63→```
    64→
    65→## Unit of Behavior (not Unit of Code)
    66→
    67→Test = story about the problem your code solves. Granularity related to stakeholder needs.
    68→A unit of behavior may span multiple classes. Test from driving port to driven port boundary.
    69→Key question: "Can you explain this test to a stakeholder?" If not, you're testing implementation details.
    70→
    71→## Classical vs Mockist Verification
    72→
    73→Classical TDD: real objects | state verification | less coupled to implementation | survives refactoring better.
    74→Mockist TDD: mocks for objects with behavior | behavior verification | lighter setup | more coupled to impl.
    75→Best practice: combine strategically. Behavior verification at layer boundaries, state verification within layers.
    76→
    77→## Test Doubles Taxonomy (Meszaros)
    78→- Dummy: passed but never used
    79→- Fake: working impl with shortcuts (in-memory DB)
    80→- Stub: predefined answers
    81→- Spy: stub that records interactions
    82→- Mock: pre-programmed with expectations for behavior verification
    83→
    84→Choose type by need: mock for interaction design | stub when don't care about interaction | fake for integration bridge.
    85→
    86→## Hexagonal Architecture Testing Strategy
    87→
    88→### Domain Layer
    89→Tested indirectly through driving port (application service) unit tests with real domain objects.
    90→Domain entities, value objects, domain services are implementation details. Testing them directly couples tests to internal structure.
    91→
    92→Exception: Standalone domain logic with complex algorithms (e.g., pricing engine) MAY be tested directly when complexity warrants it and the class has a stable public interface. This is the EXCEPTION, not the rule.
    93→
    94→### Application Layer
    95→Classical TDD within layer, Mockist TDD at port boundaries.
    96→Use real Order, Money, Customer objects in application service tests.
    97→Mock IPaymentGateway, IEmailService ports when testing orchestration.
    98→
    99→### Infrastructure Layer
   100→Integration tests ONLY - no unit tests for adapters.
   101→Mocking infrastructure inside an adapter test is testing the mock, not the adapter.
   102→Use real infrastructure (testcontainers, in-memory databases) to verify actual behavior.
   103→
   104→### E2E Tests
   105→Minimal mocking - only truly external systems (3rd party APIs beyond your control).
   106→Use real domain services, application services, repositories.
   107→
   108→## Test Doubles Policy
   109→
   110→Acceptable (port boundaries only):
   111→- `Mock<IPaymentGateway>` - external payment service port
   112→- `Mock<IEmailService>` - external email provider port
   113→- `InMemoryUserRepository` - fake for fast tests (implements IUserRepository port)
   114→
   115→Do not mock inside the hexagon:
   116→- Domain entities (Order, Customer) - use real objects
   117→- Value objects (Money, Email) - cheap to create, deterministic
   118→- Application services (OrderProcessor) - use real with mocked ports
   119→- Domain services (PricingService) - use real objects
   120→
   121→## Walking Skeleton Protocol
   122→
   123→At most one walking skeleton per new feature. When `is_walking_skeleton: true` in roadmap:
   124→- Write exactly ONE E2E/acceptance test proving end-to-end wiring
   125→- Implement thinnest possible slice - hardcoded values, minimal branching
   126→- Do NOT write unit tests - the E2E test IS the deliverable
   127→- Do NOT add error handling, edge cases, or validation
   128→- Skip inner TDD loop, go directly from RED_ACCEPTANCE to GREEN
   129→
   130→E2E tests are inherently slow and flaky. The walking skeleton is the ONE justified E2E test per feature.
   131→
   132→## E2E Test Management
   133→
   134→Enable ONE E2E test at a time to prevent commit blocks:
   135→1. All E2E tests except first one marked with skip/ignore
   136→2. Complete first scenario through Outside-In TDD
   137→3. Commit working implementation
   138→4. Enable next E2E test
   139→5. Repeat until all scenarios implemented
   140→
   141→## Step Method Pattern
   142→
   143→Step methods call production services, not test infrastructure:
   144→
   145→```csharp
   146→[When("business action occurs")]
   147→public async Task WhenBusinessActionOccurs()
   148→{
   149→    var service = _serviceProvider.GetRequiredService<IBusinessService>();
   150→    _result = await service.PerformBusinessActionAsync(_testData);
   151→}
   152→```
   153→
   154→Scaffold unimplemented collaborators with `NotImplementedException`:
   155→```csharp
   156→throw new NotImplementedException(
   157→    "Business capability not yet implemented - driven by outside-in TDD"
   158→);
   159→```
   160→
   161→## Business-Focused Testing
   162→
   163→### Unit Test Naming
   164→- Class pattern: `<DrivingPort>Should`
   165→- Method pattern: `<ExpectedOutcome>_When<SpecificBehavior>[_Given<Preconditions>]`
   166→- Example: `AccountServiceShould.IncreaseBalance_WhenDepositMade_GivenSufficientFunds`
   167→
   168→### Behavior Types
   169→- Command behavior: changes system state (Given-When-Then)
   170→- Query behavior: returns state projection (Given-Then)
   171→- Process behavior: orchestrates multiple commands/queries
   172→
   173→### Test Structure
   174→- Arrange: set up business context and test data
   175→- Act: perform business action
   176→- Assert: validate business outcome and state changes
   177→
   178→## Environment-Adaptive Testing
   179→- Local development: in-memory infrastructure for fast feedback (~100ms)
   180→- CI/CD pipeline: production-like infrastructure for integration validation (~2-5s)
   181→- Same scenarios: single source of truth across all environments
   182→

1→# FP Hexagonal Architecture
     2→
     3→Ports and adapters in functional programming. Structure applications with a pure core and side-effect shell.
     4→
     5→Cross-references: [fp-principles](./fp-principles.md) | [fp-domain-modeling](./fp-domain-modeling.md) | [fp-usable-design](./fp-usable-design.md)
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. The Natural Fit
    10→
    11→[STARTER]
    12→
    13→Functional architecture naturally implements ports and adapters. The paradigm's separation of pure functions from side effects IS the hexagonal boundary.
    14→
    15→| OOP Concept | FP Equivalent | Why |
    16→|---|---|---|
    17→| Port (interface) | Function type signature / type alias | Port defines contract; function signature IS that contract |
    18→| Adapter (class) | Concrete function implementation | Adapter fulfills contract; matching function does same |
    19→| DI container | Function parameters / partial application | Dependencies passed as arguments, no container needed |
    20→| Domain service class | Module of pure functions | Related pure functions replace stateful service object |
    21→| Entity with behavior | Immutable data + functions operating on it | Data and behavior separated; functions transform immutable values |
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## 2. Pure Core / Side-Effect Shell
    26→
    27→[STARTER]
    28→
    29→All business logic is pure; all side effects live at the system's edges.
    30→
    31→**The Sandwich Pattern**: Read (impure) -> Decide (pure) -> Write (impure)
    32→
    33→```
    34→+--------------------------------------------------+
    35→|  Side-Effect Shell (thin)                        |
    36→|  - HTTP handlers, CLI, message consumers         |
    37→|  - Database access, file I/O, network calls      |
    38→|  - Reads data, calls core, writes results        |
    39→|                                                  |
    40→|  +--------------------------------------------+ |
    41→|  |  Pure Core (large)                          | |
    42→|  |  - Pure functions only                      | |
    43→|  |  - Domain logic, validation, calculation    | |
    44→|  |  - No I/O, no side effects                  | |
    45→|  |  - Immutable data transformations           | |
    46→|  +--------------------------------------------+ |
    47→+--------------------------------------------------+
    48→```
    49→
    50→**Dependency Rule**: Shell may call core. Core never calls shell. Core is unaware of shell's existence.
    51→
    52→**Why**: Pure core is trivially testable (no mocks, no setup, no teardown). Shell is thin and needs few integration tests.
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## 3. Ports as Function Types
    57→
    58→[STARTER]
    59→
    60→A port is a function type signature describing a capability the domain needs:
    61→
    62→```
    63→FindOrder    : OrderId -> AsyncResult<Order option>
    64→SaveOrder    : Order -> AsyncResult<unit>
    65→SendEmail    : Email -> AsyncResult<unit>
    66→GetPrice     : ProductCode -> Price
    67→CheckExists  : ProductCode -> bool
    68→```
    69→
    70→**When to define**: Domain needs a capability involving I/O or external systems. Domain declares WHAT; adapter provides HOW.
    71→
    72→**Naming**: Verb-noun. Name describes capability, not technology.
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## 4. Adapters as Implementations
    77→
    78→[STARTER]
    79→
    80→An adapter is a concrete function matching a port's type signature:
    81→
    82→```
    83→PostgresOrderRepo.findOrder  : OrderId -> AsyncResult<Order option>
    84→InMemoryOrderRepo.findOrder  : OrderId -> AsyncResult<Order option>
    85→```
    86→
    87→Both match the `FindOrder` port. Domain doesn't know which is used.
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## 5. Dependency Injection via Functions
    92→
    93→[STARTER] -> [INTERMEDIATE] -> [ADVANCED]
    94→
    95→### Decision Tree: How to Inject This Dependency?
    96→
    97→```
    98→How many dependencies does the function need?
    99→  1-3 --> [STARTER] Functions as Parameters
   100→  4-6 --> [INTERMEDIATE] Consider Environment Pattern or grouping
   101→  7+  --> [ADVANCED] Capability Interfaces or Effect System
   102→         (also: reconsider function responsibilities)
   103→```
   104→
   105→### [STARTER] Functions as Parameters
   106→
   107→Pass dependencies as function parameters. Partially apply at composition root.
   108→
   109→```
   110→placeOrder (findCustomer) (saveOrder) (rawOrder) = ...
   111→placeOrderHandler = placeOrder Database.findCustomer Database.saveOrder
   112→```
   113→
   114→### [INTERMEDIATE] Environment Pattern (Reader)
   115→
   116→Dependencies in a record, provided once at top level. Use when parameter threading becomes painful (4+ deps).
   117→
   118→```
   119→placeOrder (rawOrder) = reader { env = ask(); env.findCustomer(rawOrder.customerId) ... }
   120→placeOrder(rawOrder) |> runWith(productionEnv)
   121→```
   122→
   123→### [ADVANCED] Capability Interfaces / Effect Systems
   124→
   125→Abstract over effect types (tagless final) or use fine-grained effect tracking (ZIO, Koka). Use for large codebases with many effects.
   126→
   127→### Recommendation by Context
   128→
   129→| Context | Approach |
   130→|---|---|
   131→| Small/medium codebase | Functions as parameters |
   132→| Large codebase, many effects | Capability interfaces or effect system |
   133→| Pragmatic TypeScript/F# | Functions as parameters + modules |
   134→
   135→---
   136→
   137→## 6. Pipeline Composition Through Architecture
   138→
   139→[INTERMEDIATE]
   140→
   141→Workflows flow through architecture as pipelines:
   142→
   143→```
   144→HTTP Request
   145→  -> Parse (shell: impure)
   146→  -> Validate (core: pure)
   147→  -> Calculate (core: pure)
   148→  -> Persist (shell: impure)
   149→  -> Respond (shell: impure)
   150→```
   151→
   152→Each pure step is a function in the pipeline. Shell handles I/O at start and end.
   153→
   154→**Error-track pipelines**: Each step returns Result type; pipeline short-circuits on first failure. See [fp-domain-modeling](./fp-domain-modeling.md).
   155→
   156→**Collect-all-errors**: When you need ALL validation errors, use Applicative style. See [fp-principles](./fp-principles.md) section 5.
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→## 7. Testing Strategy
   161→
   162→[INTERMEDIATE]
   163→
   164→| Layer | Test Type | Volume | Speed | Mocks |
   165→|---|---|---|---|---|
   166→| Pure core (domain) | Unit + Property-based | Many | Fast (ms) | None |
   167→| Composition root | Integration (wiring) | Few | Medium | None |
   168→| Adapters | Integration | Few per adapter | Slow | None (real deps) |
   169→| End-to-end | System tests | Very few | Slowest | None |
   170→
   171→**Key insight**: Pure functions need no mocking. Input in, output out. Strongest practical argument for maximizing the pure core.
   172→
   173→**Property-based testing** is the natural companion. Define rules that hold for all valid inputs. See [fp-algebra-driven-design](./fp-algebra-driven-design.md).
   174→
   175→---
   176→
   177→## 8. Side Effect Management Approaches
   178→
   179→[ADVANCED]
   180→
   181→| Approach | Enforcement | Granularity | Best For |
   182→|---|---|---|---|
   183→| Convention (discipline) | None | N/A | Any language, small teams |
   184→| IO Type (Haskell) | Compile-time | Binary (pure/impure) | Haskell |
   185→| Effect Systems (ZIO, Koka) | Compile-time | Per-effect | Large systems |
   186→| Pure Core / Shell | Architectural | Module-level | Any language, pragmatic |
   187→
   188→**IO actions as values**: Side effects are descriptions of actions, not actions themselves. Can be stored, composed, and only execute when runtime reaches them.
   189→
   190→**Type-level effect tracking**: Mark impure functions clearly -- through return types, naming conventions, or annotations. Even without compiler enforcement, the discipline applies.
   191→
   192→---
   193→
   194→## 9. Combining Patterns
   195→
   196→```
   197→Domain Wrappers + Smart Constructors (fp-domain-modeling)
   198→        |
   199→        v
   200→Choice Types for State Machines -----> Error-Track Pipelines
   201→        |                                       |
   202→        v                                       v
   203→Pure Core / Side-Effect Shell ---------> Functions as Parameters (DI)
   204→        |                                       |
   205→        v                                       v
   206→Pipeline Composition <-----------------  Property-Based Testing
   207→```
   208→
   209→### Worked Example: Place Order Workflow
   210→
   211→```
   212→-- Ports (function signatures)
   213→FindCustomer : CustomerId -> AsyncResult<Customer>
   214→SaveOrder    : Order -> AsyncResult<Unit>
   215→
   216→-- Pure Core (domain logic)
   217→validateOrder : RawOrder -> Result<ValidOrder, ValidationError>
   218→priceOrder    : ValidOrder -> PricedOrder
   219→
   220→-- Pipeline (Pure Core + Error Pipeline + DI via parameters)
   221→placeOrder (findCustomer) (saveOrder) (raw) =
   222→    raw
   223→    |> validateOrder                     -- pure, Result
   224→    |> bindAsync (o -> findCustomer o.customerId |> map (c -> (o, c)))  -- port call
   225→    |> map (fun (o, c) -> priceOrder o)  -- pure
   226→    |> bindAsync saveOrder               -- port call
   227→```
   228→
   229→**Recommended learning sequence**:
   230→
   231→[STARTER]: Pure Core/Shell -> Domain Wrappers -> Smart Constructors -> Pipeline Composition
   232→
   233→[INTERMEDIATE]: Choice Types -> Error-Track Pipelines -> Functions as Parameters -> Property Testing
   234→
   235→[ADVANCED]: Capability Interfaces -> Effect Systems -> Collect-All-Errors Validation
   236→

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(orchestrator): wire event stream into session lifecycle

Add startEventIteration function that bridges the OpenCode event stream
with the event bridge and stall detector. Events flow through the bridge
to SSE clients, session transitions to active on first event, and
iteration stops on terminal status or stream error.

- EventIterationDeps port defines injectable dependencies (emitEvent,
  updateSessionStatus, getSessionStatus, startStallDetector)
- Pure event bridge handles transform + forwarding + stall notification
- Error in event stream transitions session to error status
- Stall detector starts on iteration begin, stops on iteration end
- 7 unit tests covering all acceptance criteria

Step-ID: 01-02
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Description: Store sendPrompt in the existing handleRegistry (alongside abort). Add POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt route that validates session is active and forwards text via the registered handle.

    51→| 2 | Send prompt to idle agent (reactivates) | Happy | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    52→| 3 | User prompt echoed in event stream | Happy | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    53→| 4 | Prompt rejected for completed session | Error | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    54→| 5 | Prompt rejected for aborted session | Error | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    55→| 6 | Prompt rejected for error session | Error | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    56→| 7 | Empty prompt text rejected | Edge | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    57→| 8 | Prompt to nonexistent session fails | Edge | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    58→| 9 | Prompt fails when handle missing | Edge | follow-up-prompt.test.ts |
    59→
    60→### US-CS-004: Contextual Review (9 scenarios)
    61→
    62→| # | Scenario | Type | File |
    63→|---|----------|------|------|
    64→| 1 | Review provides conversation log chronologically | Happy | contextual-review.test.ts |
    65→| 2 | User prompts as distinct entries in log | Happy | contextual-review.test.ts |
    66→| 3 | File change notifications in log | Happy | contextual-review.test.ts |
    67→| 4 | Review includes session metadata | Happy | contextual-review.test.ts |
    68→| 5 | Log without user prompts (agent-only) | Happy | contextual-review.test.ts |
    69→| 6 | Log for nonexistent session fails | Error | contextual-review.test.ts |
    70→| 7 | Log available for aborted session | Edge | contextual-review.test.ts |
    71→| 8 | Reject feedback delivered, session resumes | Happy | contextual-review.test.ts |
    72→| 9 | Log includes rejection feedback as prompt | Edge | contextual-review.test.ts |
    73→
    74→## Acceptance Criteria Coverage Matrix
    75→
    76→| AC Description | Scenario(s) |
    77→|---------------|-------------|
    78→| Event iteration starts after spawn | WS-1, ESW-1 |
    79→| Events piped through event bridge to SSE | WS-1, ESW-1 |
    80→| Stall detector starts after bridge wired | ESW-3 |
    81→| Iteration stops on terminal status | ESW-5, ESW-6 |
    82→| Stream errors update session to error | WS-3, ESW-4 |
    83→| Session active after first event | ESW-2 |
    84→| Token events stream into output | LAO-1 |
    85→| File changes as inline notifications | LAO-2 |
    86→| Status badge real-time updates | LAO-3 |
    87→| Files changed count updates | LAO-4 |
    88→| Stall warning after 30s inactivity | LAO-5 |
    89→| Prompt delivers via POST (202) | WS-1, FUP-1 |
    90→| Prompt to idle transitions to active | FUP-2 |
    91→| User messages as distinct blocks | FUP-3 |
    92→| Input disabled for completed session | FUP-4 |
    93→| Input disabled for aborted session | FUP-5 |
    94→| Input disabled for error session | FUP-6 |
    95→| Prompt fails when handle missing | FUP-9 |
    96→| Agent Log chronological trail | WS-2, CR-1 |
    97→| User prompts distinct from agent output | CR-2 |
    98→| File changes inline in log | CR-3 |
    99→| Session metadata in review header | CR-4 |
   100→| Reject with feedback resumes agent | CR-8 |
   101→
   102→## Driving Ports Used
   103→
   104→All tests invoke through these HTTP endpoints (driving ports):
   105→
   106→- `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/assign` -- session creation
   107→- `GET  /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id` -- session status
   108→- `GET  /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/stream` -- SSE event stream
   109→- `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt` -- follow-up prompt (NEW)
   110→- `GET  /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/log` -- conversation log (NEW)
   111→- `GET  /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/review` -- review summary
   112→- `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/accept` -- accept work
   113→- `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/reject` -- reject with feedback
   114→- `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/abort` -- abort session
   115→
   116→## Property-Shaped Scenarios
   117→
   118→| Scenario | Signal | File |
   119→|----------|--------|------|
   120→| Event ordering preserved regardless of rate | ordering guarantee | live-agent-output.feature |
   121→
   122→## Implementation Sequence (One-at-a-Time)
   123→
   124→1. WS-1: Assign, observe, send follow-up (ENABLED)
   125→2. ESW-1: Agent events flow to stream
   126→3. ESW-2: Session transitions to active
   127→4. FUP-1: Send prompt to active agent
   128→5. FUP-4: Prompt rejected for completed session
   129→6. FUP-7: Empty prompt rejected
   130→7. FUP-8: Prompt to nonexistent session
   131→8. ESW-4: Stream error updates session
   132→9. ESW-5: Iteration stops on completed
   133→10. ESW-7: Nonexistent stream subscription
   134→11. CR-1: Log in chronological order
   135→12. CR-2: User prompts distinct in log
   136→13. CR-6: Log for nonexistent session
   137→14. (remaining scenarios by feature area)
   138→
   139→## Mandate Compliance Evidence
   140→
   141→**CM-A (Driving port usage)**: All test files import from `coding-session-test-kit.ts` which re-exports from `orchestrator-test-kit.ts`. Tests invoke through HTTP endpoints only. Zero internal component imports.
   142→
   143→**CM-B (Business language purity)**: Gherkin uses domain terms: "Marcus", "coding agent", "follow-up prompt", "conversation log", "session", "review". Zero technical terms (no HTTP, JSON, API, database, SSE in Gherkin).
   144→
   145→**CM-C (Walking skeleton + focused counts)**: 3 walking skeletons + 28 focused scenarios = 31 total. Error/edge ratio: 52%.
   146→
